players.name: Lionel Mpasi
players.bio: Meaux-born shot-stopper who made his name in Ligue 2 before earning his Léopards stripes. With 29 caps, Mpasi is Congo DR's trusted last line of defence heading into his first World Cup.
players.facts: Born in France, plays in France, but flies the Congolese flag — a true product of the diaspora generation.

players.name: Aaron Wan-Bissaka
players.bio: The spider-legged defender famous for his jaw-dropping 1v1 defending. After starring at Man United, Wan-Bissaka chose Congo DR over England and brings elite Premier League experience to this squad.
players.facts: His tackle success rate at Man United was so high, fans genuinely debated whether tackling should be renamed 'Wan-Bissakaing'.

players.name: Steve Kapuadi
players.bio: A composed centre-back born in Saint-Denis who carved his career through French football before heading east to Poland. Kapuadi is still building his international résumé with just 4 caps to his name.
players.facts: He plays club football in Łódź, Poland — a city name that confuses spell-checkers and commentators in equal measure.

players.name: Axel Tuanzebe
players.bio: Born in Bunia, DR Congo, and raised in England, Tuanzebe is a physically imposing defender who came through Manchester United's academy. A rare Congolese-born player in this heavily diaspora squad.
players.facts: He's one of very few players at this World Cup to have been born in DR Congo and play for a Premier League club.

players.name: Dylan Batubinsika
players.bio: A rangy, athletic centre-back born near Paris who has bounced across European leagues. Batubinsika brings leadership and versatility to the Léopards' backline with 14 caps under his belt.
players.facts: He has played club football in France, Scotland, and Greece — a proper football nomad collecting passport stamps.

players.name: Ngal'ayel Mukau
players.bio: One of the most exciting young midfielders in Ligue 1, Mukau is just 21 and already a key cog at Lille. The Antwerp-born enforcer is the engine room of Congo DR's midfield at his debut World Cup.
players.facts: Born in Belgium, developed in France, plays for Congo DR — and is still only 21. The future is very much now.

players.name: Nathanaël Mbuku
players.bio: A pacey, direct winger with flair to burn, Mbuku has been a bright spark for Montpellier in Ligue 1. Still only 24, he brings creativity and directness to the Léopards' attacking play on the big stage.
players.facts: Mbuku was born in 2002, meaning he was still in primary school when some of his international teammates turned professional.

players.name: Samuel Moutoussamy
players.bio: A tireless box-to-box midfielder with nearly 60 caps, Moutoussamy is one of Congo DR's most experienced campaigners. A reliable presence who keeps the engine running whether the match is tense or comfortable.
players.facts: With 58 caps and 0 international goals, Moutoussamy has attended more Léopards parties than anyone — just never scored the cake.

players.name: Brian Cipenga
players.bio: A forward who has plied his trade in Spanish football with Castellón, Cipenga is still hunting his first international goal. An energetic striker who offers pace and pressing from the front.
players.facts: Eight caps and no goals for Congo DR — but at a World Cup, any one moment can change a career forever.

players.name: Théo Bongonda
players.bio: Born in Charleroi, the pacy winger chose Congo DR over Belgium and has been a consistent attacking threat. Bongonda's direct running and seven international goals make him a genuine danger man.
players.facts: He once played in Russia's Premier League with Spartak Moscow — one of the more exotic destinations for a Belgian-Congolese winger.

players.name: Gaël Kakuta
players.bio: A mercurial talent who once trained with Chelsea as a teenager, Kakuta has had a remarkable career journey. Now 34, the Lille-born playmaker is the creative elder statesman of this Congo DR side.
players.facts: Chelsea were once fined by FIFA for signing Kakuta illegally as a youth player. He's been causing chaos since before he turned pro.

players.name: Joris Kayembe
players.bio: Brussels-born left-back who brings attacking intent and European experience from his time at Genk. Kayembe has been a dependable servant to the Léopards with 26 caps and a knack for getting forward.
players.facts: Joris and Edo Kayembe share a surname but are not brothers — just two Kayembes making Congo DR's squad even more confusing for commentators.

players.name: Meschak Elia
players.bio: Kinshasa's own, Elia is one of the few locally-born stars in this squad and one of its most experienced with 69 caps. A dynamic attacking midfielder with 12 goals, he's a fan favourite and creative spark.
players.facts: Born and raised in Kinshasa, Elia represents the heart of Congolese football in a squad largely built from the European diaspora.

players.name: Noah Sadiki
players.bio: A composed, intelligent central midfielder who joined Sunderland after impressing in Belgium. At just 21, Sadiki is one of the youngest players in this squad and is gaining experience fast.
players.facts: Sadiki was born in December 2004 — meaning he shares a birth year with some players still in youth academies at this World Cup.

players.name: Aaron Tshibola
players.bio: A hard-working midfielder who has represented Congo DR 17 times, Tshibola brings graft and experience from his career in English and Scottish football. A dependable squad option with an eye for goal.
players.facts: Tshibola played in the English Championship and is now at Kilmarnock — a career spanning the full British footballing spectrum.

players.name: Timothy Fayulu
players.bio: A young goalkeeper with just 3 caps, Fayulu is the third-choice stopper in this squad. Plying his trade at Armenian club Noah, he's here to learn and absorb the World Cup experience.
players.facts: Timothy Fayulu plays for a club called Noah — presumably the one place where every creature is welcome, including backup goalkeepers.

players.name: Cédric Bakambu
players.bio: A veteran striker with 70 caps and 21 goals, Bakambu is the Léopards' most decorated forward. At 35, this World Cup may be his last dance, and the former Villarreal man still knows where the net is.
players.facts: Bakambu once moved to Beijing Guoan for a reported African record transfer fee. He truly has gone where the money — and the adventure — is.

players.name: Charles Pickel
players.bio: Despite the royal surname, Pickel is strictly a footballing man. The industrious midfielder has made over 30 appearances for Congo DR and brings grit and organisation to the engine room from his Spanish base at Espanyol.
players.facts: His surname is Pickel. He plays in Spain. The matchday programme writers had to try very hard not to make a joke here.

players.name: Fiston Mayele
players.bio: A reliable striker who has found his home at Pyramids FC in Egypt, Mayele brings physicality and a nose for goal to Congo DR's attack. Six international goals across 37 caps tells a story of consistent contribution.
players.facts: Mayele scores goals in Egypt while playing for a club literally named Pyramids. Historically significant in more ways than one.

players.name: Yoane Wissa
players.bio: The Léopards' star man. Wissa's electric pace, intelligent movement, and nine international goals have made him Newcastle's cult hero and Congo DR's talisman. Born in France, he's fully Congolese at heart.
players.facts: Wissa banged in goals for Brentford before earning his big move to Newcastle — he's been one of the Premier League's most underrated forwards.

players.name: Matthieu Epolo
players.bio: A teenage goalkeeper born in Brussels, Epolo is one of the youngest players at the tournament. His World Cup debut squad call-up comes just one cap into his international career — the world is his oyster.
players.facts: Epolo was born in 2005, making him young enough that several of his teammates were already professionals when he started secondary school.

players.name: Chancel Mbemba
players.bio: The Léopards' most capped player with over 100 appearances, Mbemba is a colossus. Born in what was then Zaire, the Lille defender is the heartbeat of this defence and a towering figure in Congolese football history.
players.facts: Mbemba was born in Zaire — the country that became Congo DR in 1997. He's literally as old as the nation's modern identity.

players.name: Simon Banza
players.bio: A powerful centre-forward born in Creil, France, Banza chose Congo DR and has been a useful option with 15 caps. His stint in the UAE with Al Jazira keeps him sharp and scoring regularly.
players.facts: Simon Banza is the rare player who swapped European football for the UAE — and still ended up at a World Cup.

players.name: Gédéon Kalulu
players.bio: Lyon-born right-back and younger brother of AC Milan's Pierre Kalulu, Gédéon has carved his own path. His 28 caps show he's earned his place in the Léopards' squad on merit, not just the family name.
players.facts: His brother Pierre Kalulu plays for AC Milan. The Kalulu family is building quite the football dynasty — one sibling at a time.

players.name: Edo Kayembe
players.bio: Born in Kananga, DR Congo, and developed through Belgian football, Kayembe is a combative defensive midfielder with 43 caps. A no-nonsense presence who breaks up play and keeps things ticking at Watford.
players.facts: Edo Kayembe was born in Congo, plays in England, and shares a surname with Joris Kayembe — proof that Congo DR contains multitudes.

players.name: Arthur Masuaku
players.bio: A dynamic, attack-minded left-back or winger with 45 caps and four international goals, Masuaku adds flair down the flank. A veteran of West Ham and now Lens, he's one of Congo DR's most versatile weapons.
players.facts: Masuaku once scored a bizarre flick for West Ham that went viral worldwide — proof that even accidental genius deserves applause.
